source: Multiple sources: Malchiodi, C. (2011). Handbook of Art Therapy; Levine, S. & Levine, E. (1999). Foundations of Expressive Arts Therapy.
description: Creative and expressive arts practices for emotional processing, self-discovery, and healing. Art-making provides non-verbal channels for expression, particularly valuable when words are insufficient or unavailable.

strategies.name: Free-Form Art Making (Process over Product)
strategies.description: Create art without judgment or goal, allowing emotions to flow through the creative process.
steps: Gather materials: paper, paints, markers, clay, collage materials - whatever calls to you
Set intention: 'I'm making space for whatever wants to emerge'
Let go of 'making something good' - this is process, not product
Check in: What am I feeling right now?
Choose colors, textures, movements that match or express the feeling
Let your hands move without planning or thinking
Don't censor - messy, ugly, chaotic is all welcome
If stuck, just make marks - scribbles, lines, shapes
Let the art evolve - it may surprise you
Work for 15-30 minutes or until it feels complete
Step back and look at what emerged
Notice: What do I see? What do I feel looking at this?
You don't need to analyze it - just witness what came through
Option: Write a few sentences about the experience

citations.author: McNiff, S.
citations.title: Art Heals: How Creativity Cures the Soul

strategies.contraindications: May bring up strong emotions. Have support available if working with trauma.

strategies.name: Movement/Dance as Emotional Expression
strategies.description: Use free-form movement to express and release emotions that are difficult to verbalize.
steps: Create private space, choose music that resonates with your mood
Start standing, close eyes, take a few breaths
Scan body: Where do I feel emotion? What wants to move?
Begin moving that part - let it lead
Let movement be authentic: Angry? Stomp, punch air. Sad? Collapse, curl. Joy? Jump, spin.
No choreography, no 'looking good' - this is YOUR movement
Let sound come out: sighs, groans, cries, laughter
Exaggerate the movement - make it BIG
Notice what shifts: Does sadness become anger? Does anger soften to grief?
Follow those shifts with your body
Dance for 10-30 minutes
Gradually slow down, come to stillness
Lie down and rest, noticing body sensations
Journal briefly if words want to come

citations.author: Levine, P. & Levine, E.
citations.title: Foundations of Expressive Arts Therapy: Theoretical and Clinical Perspectives

citations.author: Roth, G.
citations.title: Sweat Your Prayers: The Five Rhythms of the Soul

strategies.contraindications: Requires privacy. May be activating - have grounding techniques ready.

strategies.name: Music for Mood Regulation
strategies.description: Intentionally use music to shift emotional state, either by matching mood and allowing processing, or by guiding toward desired state.
steps: TWO APPROACHES:
APPROACH 1 - ISO Principle (Match then Shift):
Start with music that MATCHES your current mood
Sad? Start with sad music. Angry? Start with intense music.
This validates and allows the emotion
After 10-15 minutes, gradually shift to slightly more uplifting music
Continue shifting playlist toward desired emotional state
End with music that embodies how you want to feel
APPROACH 2 - Direct Shift:
Choose music that embodies desired state
Uplifting, energizing music if feeling down
Calming music if feeling anxious
Listen actively - don't just have it in background
Let yourself be moved by it
Sing along, move to it, feel it in your body
20-30 minutes of intentional listening

citations.author: Saarikallio, S.
citations.title: Music as emotional self-regulation throughout adulthood

citations.author: Thoma, M. et al.
citations.title: The Effect of Music on the Human Stress Response

strategies.contraindications: Avoid music associated with trauma. Be mindful of songs that might trigger difficult memories.

strategies.name: Therapeutic Journaling Variations
strategies.description: Structured journaling prompts beyond free-writing for emotional processing and insight.
steps: Choose a prompt that resonates:
UNSENT LETTER: Write to someone (living or dead) saying everything unsaid. Don't send it.
DIALOGUE: Have a written conversation between parts of yourself or with your emotion
GRATITUDE FOCUS: '3 good things' from today, with details about why each mattered
FUTURE SELF: Write from your future self to your current self with compassion and wisdom
WORST CASE/BEST CASE: Explore both extremes, then most likely realistic outcome
TIMELINE: Map your emotional journey over a day/week/year visually
METAPHOR: 'My [emotion] is like...' and explore the metaphor
Write for 15-20 minutes without stopping
Don't edit or censor - just let it flow
Re-read when complete - notice insights
Optional: Destroy it after (especially cathartic for anger/grief)
Or keep it as a record of your emotional process

citations.author: Pennebaker, J.W.
citations.title: Writing About Emotional Experiences as a Therapeutic Process

citations.author: Smyth, J.M.
citations.title: Written emotional expression: effect sizes, outcome types, and moderating variables

strategies.contraindications: May intensify emotions initially. Not a substitute for therapy with severe trauma.

strategies.name: Poetry as Emotional Processing
strategies.description: Use poetic language and structure to give form to emotional experience, making the ineffable tangible.
steps: No experience needed - this isn't about 'good poetry'
APPROACH 1 - Found Poetry:
Take a journal entry or piece of writing
Circle powerful words and phrases
Arrange them into lines - a poem emerges
APPROACH 2 - 'I Am' Poem:
I am... (two characteristics)
I wonder... I hear... I see... I want...
I am... (repeat line 1)
Fill in with your authentic experience
APPROACH 3 - Free-Verse:
Write without rhyme or structure
Use metaphor, imagery, sensory details
Break lines where it feels right
Let rhythm emerge naturally
Don't explain - SHOW through images
Read it aloud - hear your own voice
Notice what it's like to give form to formless feeling

citations.author: Hynes, A. & Hynes-Berry, M.
citations.title: Biblio/Poetry Therapy: The Interactive Process: A Handbook

strategies.contraindications: May feel uncomfortable if new to creative writing. Remember: there's no 'wrong' way.

strategies.name: Collage as Subconscious Expression
strategies.description: Create meaning through selecting and arranging images, bypassing verbal/analytical mind.
steps: Gather: magazines, printed images, scissors, glue, paper/cardboard base
Set loose intention: 'How I feel now', 'Where I want to go', 'My inner world'
Flip through magazines quickly
Tear/cut images that resonate - don't think, just follow the pull
Include words, textures, colors that call to you
Doesn't have to 'make sense' logically
Once you have a pile, begin arranging on your base
Overlap, layer, tear, reconstruct
Let it evolve - trust the process
When it feels complete, glue everything down
Step back and look at what you created
What do you notice? What surprises you?
Often the collage reveals things you didn't consciously know
Write a title or a few sentences if words emerge
Keep it somewhere you can see it - it may reveal more over time
strategies.contraindications: Triggering images in magazines - be mindful. Have grounding available.

meta.evidence_base: Expressive arts therapies are increasingly recognized as effective adjunct treatments for trauma, depression, anxiety, and emotional processing. Pennebaker's research on expressive writing shows strong evidence. Art therapy, music therapy, and dance/movement therapy have growing bodies of clinical and research support. Particularly valuable when verbal processing is difficult or insufficient.
meta.target_populations: Universal - everyone can benefit from creative expression. Particularly valuable for trauma survivors (who may lack words), children and adolescents, those who are 'tired of talking', people who feel emotions in their body, and anyone seeking non-verbal processing channels.
meta.core_principle: Creativity is innate. Art-making isn't about talent or creating 'good art' - it's about the PROCESS of externalizing internal experience. Making something physical from something emotional creates distance, perspective, and allows witnessing of one's own experience. As the poet Rainer Maria Rilke wrote: 'Perhaps all the dragons in our lives are princesses who are only waiting to see us act, just once, with beauty and courage.'
meta.access_note: Creative expression requires no special equipment or training. Simple materials (paper, pen, markers, magazines, device for music) are sufficient. The barrier is often permission - giving yourself permission to create 'badly', to be messy, to let something emerge without controlling it.
meta.integration: Creative practices work beautifully alongside talk therapy, mindfulness, and body-based work. They can access different parts of experience and memory. Many therapists integrate expressive arts into trauma treatment specifically because it engages non-verbal, right-brain processing.
meta.training_note: While these can be practiced solo, working with a certified art therapist, music therapist, or expressive arts therapist can deepen the process, especially for complex trauma or mental health concerns.
